raw_data:
b' Money Stuff
The general rule in mergers and acquisitions is that if you want to buy a company you have to pay all the shareholders the same price. This

Turquoise Hill

The general rule in mergers and acquisitions is that if you want to buy a company you have to pay all the shareholders the same price. This is not an absolute rule. You can buy some shares of the company at varying\xc2\xa0market prices before you decide to buy the whole thing at a fixed price, as Elon Musk did with Twitter Inc. [1] Shareholders of the company who are also\xc2\xa0executives can get paid more than regular shareholders, in the form of employment contracts or severance pay. Shareholders with special classes of shares that get more votes can get paid more for those votes. But for the most part you are not allowed to simply say, like, \xe2\x80\x9cwe will pay 51% of shareholders $40 per share, and the other 49% $1 per share,\xe2\x80\x9d and then get the 51% of shareholders to approve the deal and stiff the 49%. [2]

But like I said this is not an absolute rule, and sometimes you will want to give a few noisy shareholders a little nudge to get a deal done. In Musk\xe2\x80\x99s deal for Twitter, for instance, one shareholder \xe2\x80\x94 Saudi\xc2\xa0Prince Alwaleed bin Talal\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x94\xc2\xa0complained that Musk\xe2\x80\x99s bid of $54.20 per share did not \xe2\x80\x9ccome close to the intrinsic value\xe2\x80\x9d of Twitter. (This was in April, when that was a plausible position.) Alwaleed\xe2\x80\x99s Kingdom Holding Co. owned about 4.6% of Twitter, and you could imagine him voting against the deal and making it less likely to go through. Musk solved this problem by allowing Kingdom to roll its Twitter shares into the privately owned company, an offer that was extended to some other big Twitter shareholders \xe2\x80\x94\xc2\xa0including Jack Dorsey \xe2\x80\x94\xc2\xa0but not all of them. Big noisy shareholders who were either (1) friendly with Musk or (2) antagonistic to Musk in a way that might block the deal were offered the chance to keep their stock in Musk\xe2\x80\x99s private Twitter. Ordinary retail shareholders were not: They just got to vote for or against the deal, and if enough of them voted for it then they\xe2\x80\x99d all get cashed out at $54.20. They did, and they were, and given subsequent events none of them are really complaining.

In fact, this is not uncommon in going-private transactions, allowing some shareholders to roll over into the private company. If some big shareholder likes the stock and is willing to take a minority stake in the private company,\xc2\xa0that is often helpful for the acquirer, since it reduces the acquirer\xe2\x80\x99s need for cash and increases the chances of getting the deal approved.

What you can\xe2\x80\x99t really do\xc2\xa0is give a few big noisy shareholders extra cash to get them to vote for the deal. That just seems straightforwardly unfair.

On the other hand! Turquoise Hill Resources Ltd. is a Canadian mining company that is 51% owned by Rio Tinto International Holdings Ltd. Rio Tinto wants to buy the remaining 49% of Turquoise Hill for C$43 per share. Turquoise Hill\xe2\x80\x99s board appointed a special committee of independent directors, which\xc2\xa0approved the transaction at that price, and submitted it to a shareholder vote. To get approved, the deal requires (1) a two-thirds vote of all shares (which is easy since Rio owns 51%) and also (2) a majority vote of the non-Rio shareholders. Two big shareholders, Pentwater Capital Management LP and SailingStone Capital Partners LLC, don\xe2\x80\x99t like the deal and want more money. If they voted against the deal, Rio might not win.

And so Rio struck a deal with them, which Turquoise Hill somewhat passive-aggressively announced today .\xc2\xa0(Here are the actual agreements .) The shareholders agreed not to vote one way or the other on the merger, which makes it easier to get a majority vote of the non-Rio shares. (The deal\xc2\xa0only needs a majority of the non-Rio shares that vote to vote yes, so abstaining does not count as a no vote.) In exchange, Rio agreed to pay them 80% of the merger price (C$34.40) at closing, and then go to arbitration over how much the company is worth. If Rio wins the arbitration it will give them the remaining 20% (C$8.60), plus interest, meaning that they do as well as the regular shareholders who are cashed out at C$43. If Rio loses, it will pay them more, basically whatever the arbitrator decides is the fair value of Turquoise Hill.

This seems like a better deal than the public shareholders are getting. Public shareholders can either (1)\xc2\xa0approve the deal and take the C$43 with no upside, (2)\xc2\xa0reject the deal\xc2\xa0and then have minority shares in a controlled public company that are worth whatever the market says they\xe2\x80\x99re worth, or (3) approve the deal, but individual shareholders who vote no can\xc2\xa0seek \xe2\x80\x9cdissent rights\xe2\x80\x9d (called \xe2\x80\x9cappraisal rights\xe2\x80\x9d in the US), meaning that they get whatever a court decides is the fair value of the stock, but possibly less than C$43. But Pentwater and SailingStone are guaranteed C$43 and have some upside and a friendlier process for determining that upside.

That is \xe2\x80\xa6\xc2\xa0unusual, and a bit aggressive? Turquoise Hill thinks so :

The Special Committee was first advised of the potential terms of the Agreements on the evening of Sunday, October 30, 2022. The Special Committee suggested to Rio Tinto that it offer comparable dissent proceedings as those offered to the Named Shareholders in the Agreements to all holders of Minority Shares (the \xe2\x80\x9cMinority Shareholders\xe2\x80\x9d). Rio Tinto advised that it was not making the terms of the Agreements available to all Minority Shareholders.

Ah. Well. I think that if Rio just offered to pay Pentwater and Sailingstone an extra $5 per share, the special committee would have done more than \xe2\x80\x9csuggest\xe2\x80\x9d offering it to everyone. But if you punt this to arbitration where Rio might pay an extra $5 per share, that might just work.

Distress

In debt, the rule is different. We have talked a few times about what I think of as the general theory of modern distressed debt investing, and to oversimplify it only slightly, that theory is \xe2\x80\x9cpay 51% of creditors a lot of money, pay the other 49% zero, and get the 51% to vote to stiff the 49%.\xe2\x80\x9d Exactly what is not allowed in mergers. People don\xe2\x80\x99t like it that much in debt either.

The standard story goes like this. A company has a bunch of debt outstanding. It is running into trouble and needs more money to operate, or more time to pay back its debt, or both. It looks carefully into its debt documents and realizes that it can change the terms of its debt, as long as holders of a majority (or sometimes a supermajority, perhaps two-thirds) of the debt agree.

It can\xe2\x80\x99t change the terms too much \xe2\x80\x94\xc2\xa0it can\xe2\x80\x99t reduce the amount of the debt or the interest rate, or push back the maturity date, without consent from every creditor\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x94\xc2\xa0but it can do some important things. In particular, it can tinker with the seniority of the debt. A company might have secured debt outstanding that says things like \xe2\x80\x9cthis debt has a first-lien claim to all of the company\xe2\x80\x99s assets\xe2\x80\x9d and \xe2\x80\x9cthe company can\xe2\x80\x99t issue any new debt that is senior to this debt.\xe2\x80\x9d And a majority (or supermajority) vote might allow the company to move some of its assets into a new subsidiary, make that subsidiary not part of the collateral for the existing debt, and then issue new debt secured by that subsidiary\xe2\x80\x99s assets. In practice the company can issue new debt that is senior to its existing debt, even though the existing debt is supposed to be the most senior debt.

And then the company goes to some of its lenders [3] \xe2\x80\x94\xc2\xa0enough of them to vote to approve the amendments \xe2\x80\x94\xc2\xa0and says \xe2\x80\x9chey, we will create new good debt, and make our old debt bad. If you (1) vote to approve this and (2) lend us some more money, we will turn both your new money and your existing loans into good debt, and we will leave all the other lenders with the bad debt.\xe2\x80\x9d The favored lenders will get good debt in exchange for putting in more money and approving the amendments. The other lenders will watch as their good debt gets turned into bad debt.

And then one of two things happens. (Well, first the other lenders will sue, but we\xe2\x80\x99ll get to that.)

  1. Perhaps this will work. The company will survive, the extra money will be enough to fix the problems, it will avoid bankruptcy, and everyone will eventually get paid back. The favored lenders will have a better time of it, but everyone will eventually get their money.
  2. Perhaps it won\xe2\x80\x99t work and the company will go bankrupt anyway. Then the favored lenders \xe2\x80\x94\xc2\xa0whose debt is now secured by good assets \xe2\x80\x94\xc2\xa0will more or less get paid, and the disfavored lenders more or less won\xe2\x80\x99t.

Now, at this point, everyone in the market is aware of this stuff. So when a company starts running into distress, and starts making the sorts of noises and hiring the sorts of advisers that indicate it might do this sort of thing, there will be a race to buy up its debt. You want to have, or be in a group of lenders who have, a majority of the debt, so that you can be on the winning side of this trade rather than the losing side. Or if the debt requires a supermajority to do this trade, you want to acquire (or have your group of lenders acquire) a blocking position, at least one-third of the debt, so that the company can\xe2\x80\x99t do this without your permission.

One result of this is that sometimes the debt of a very distressed company will trade at very high prices \xe2\x80\x94\xc2\xa0perhaps over 100 cents on the dollar \xe2\x80\x94\xc2\xa0because that debt is in high demand to do or block this sort of transaction; it is worth overpaying for some of this debt to avoid being stiffed on\xc2\xa0the rest of your position.

Another result is that sometimes the company won\xe2\x80\x99t have a majority (or supermajority) to do the transaction: Its favored lenders will have 40%, or 55%, of the debt, and it will need 51% or 67%. But there is a fix to that: Issue more debt. If the existing debt has some provision allowing the company to issue more of it, you just issue more of it to the favored lenders, until they have 51% or 67% or whatever. Then they vote for the amendment and roll their new debt into the even newer, favored debt.

Both of these results are economically weird. A company will get into distress, its debt will trade at 40 or 60 cents on the dollar, and then it will spike up to 105 as people race to get control. And then it will fall back to 40 or 60, and the company will issue more of it at 100, as it tries to line up the right number of votes. In simple economic terms you would not pay 100 or 105 cents on the dollar for this sort of distressed debt, but to get control of a transaction like this, people do.

There are two recent lawsuits in cases like this, arguing that these transactions \xe2\x80\x94\xc2\xa0particularly, issuing new debt to get enough votes \xe2\x80\x94\xc2\xa0were \xe2\x80\x9cshams\xe2\x80\x9d and should be reversed by courts. One is about the Incora case, which we talked about back in April . (Incora is, somehow, the name of a company called Wesco Aircraft Holdings Inc.? Don\xe2\x80\x99t ask.) There, the company needed a two-thirds vote to approve changes to the debt. There were two groups of creditors, and they raced to buy the debt, causing it to trade up to 105 cents on the dollar. From the complaint :

A bidding war ensued as both sides attempted to ensure they would have the necessary amount of 2026 Senior Secured Notes to either block such a\xc2\xa0transaction in the case of Plaintiffs or form a two-thirds supermajority in the case of the Favored Noteholders. As reflected in the chart below, the 2026 Senior Secured Notes and 2024 Senior Secured Notes were both trading at roughly 85 cents on the dollar on February 7, 2022, when rumors of a potential transaction first leaked. By early March 2022, the market price of 2026 Senior Secured Notes reached 105 cents on the dollar, far above their intrinsic value.

The minority group won the race and got more than one-third of the bonds, preventing the restructuring transaction. But the company did it anyway, by issuing new notes and getting their consent:

Defendants executed (or consented to) unauthorized amendments to the Governing Indentures that resulted in the issuance of new notes to certain Favored Noteholders for the specific purpose of diluting Plaintiffs\xe2\x80\x99 blocking position in the 2026 Senior Secured Notes and obtaining a feigned supermajority. Those new notes were issued to those Favored Noteholders for no purpose other than to gerrymander a vote of the 2026 Senior Secured Notes, and once that vote occurred, the new notes were exchanged and cancelled on the same day they were issued. \xe2\x80\xa6

The fleeting existence of the Phantom Notes confirms that their only purpose was to subvert Plaintiffs\xe2\x80\x99 supermajority consent rights and illustrates the integrated nature of the agreements comprising the Sham Transaction. For example, no rational economic noteholder would have voted the Phantom Notes against their interests to immediately strip themselves of their security interests in the Collateral\xe2\x80\x94as was done through the Fourth Supplemental Indenture\xe2\x80\x94without that noteholder being assured that the Phantom Notes would immediately be exchanged for New 1L Notes. Likewise, no rational investor would have purchased the Phantom Notes but for the Company\xe2\x80\x99s promise to simultaneously exchange them for the New 1L Notes. Indeed, upon information and belief, the Phantom Notes were purchased by Favored Notedholders at or near par\xc2\xa0while otherwise identical 2026 Senior Secured Notes were trading at approximately 84 cents.

The other is about Revlon Inc., the bankrupt cosmetics maker, which we have talked about a lot because Citigroup Inc. messed up Revlon\xe2\x80\x99s restructuring\xc2\xa0transaction in an embarrassing way. But there the restructuring required majority consent, and they didn\xe2\x80\x99t have a majority, so they created one. From the complaint :

The Conspiring Lenders held only a minority of that debt\xe2\x80\x94they could not consent to an amendment on their own. And they faced united opposition from a group of more than 50% of the 2016 Term Lenders, who entered into a joint cooperation agreement and made clear they would oppose Defendants\xe2\x80\x99 proposed amendments (the \xe2\x80\x9cCo-Op Lenders\xe2\x80\x9d).

Undeterred, RCPC and the Conspiring Lenders tried to devise an end-run around the 2016 Credit Agreement\xe2\x80\x99s consent requirements: RCPC\xe2\x80\x94with Citibank\xe2\x80\x99s assistance (as Administrative and Collateral Agent)\xe2\x80\x94would issue new, unfunded revolver commitments (not real loans, just illusory commitments) under the 2016 Credit Agreement to the Conspiring Lenders. RCPC and the Conspiring Lenders then claimed that these new \xe2\x80\x9ccommitments\xe2\x80\x9d would increase the Conspiring Lenders\xe2\x80\x99 voting rights such that they would have a voting majority. And, reflecting its intent to manipulate lender voting, RCPC planned to obtain the exact amount of commitments necessary to purportedly inch over a 50.0% consent threshold. \xe2\x80\xa6

Indeed, no rational lender would have lent new bona fide money to RCPC under the 2016 Credit Agreement\xe2\x80\x94the 2016 Term Loans were trading at around 43 cents on the dollar and were certain to move much lower if the transaction went through. And, in fact, no lender did. Rather, Revlon and the Conspiring Lenders arranged for these new revolver \xe2\x80\x9cloans\xe2\x80\x9d to be replaced by the soon-to-be-issued loans under the new BrandCo debt facilities, all secured by the BrandCo IP. In fact, exactly fifteen days after they were issued, the Sham Revolver commitments disappeared. These revolver \xe2\x80\x9cloans\xe2\x80\x9d served no legitimate business purpose; rather, their sole purpose was to manipulate consent thresholds and disenfranchise the majority 2016 Term Lenders so that Defendants could consummate their scheme to divest the 2016 Term Lenders of their firstpriority liens on the BrandCo IP.

The basic question in these cases is: Can you just read the debt documents as craftily as possible, do whatever is strictly allowed by the text, and benefit some creditors at the expense of others? Or is there some background requirement of fairness or \xe2\x80\x9coh come on it can\xe2\x80\x99t have meant that,\xe2\x80\x9d so that your craftiest readings don\xe2\x80\x99t actually work? The traditional view is that shareholders are entitled to fiduciary duties \xe2\x80\x94\xc2\xa0which is why mergers have to be more or less fair to all shareholders \xe2\x80\x94\xc2\xa0while creditors are entitled only to the letter of their contract. That traditional view has given rise to, you know, all this: a whole industry of distressed-debt cleverness built on structuring transactions to exploit the documents as much as possible. I suppose it is possible to take it too far, though: If creditors get too good at ruthlessly exploiting each other, eventually courts might step in and say \xe2\x80\x9coh come on it can\xe2\x80\x99t have meant that.\xe2\x80\x9d If a rule like \xe2\x80\x9ccreditors are only entitled to what their contract explicitly says\xe2\x80\x9d always leads to absurd results, it might stop being the rule.

Borrow long to lend short

If you have a roughly-one-year-ago-vintage mortgage, you should probably never move :

Some lucky Americans are earning a higher interest rate on their high-yield savings accounts than they\xe2\x80\x99re paying on their mortgage. \xe2\x80\xa6

There were 4.2 million mortgaged homes \xe2\x80\x94 8% of the total \xe2\x80\x94 with a current rate of 2.5% or lower as of the end of September, according to Black Knight, a mortgage technology and data provider.

These people either locked in a mortgage or refinanced before rates started to surge. They\xe2\x80\x99re in the brag-worthy position of paying the same or less in interest than they could be earning on a high-yield savings account at Goldman Sachs Group Inc.\xe2\x80\x99s Marcus, which recently hiked its rate to a record 2.5%.

By comparison, the average rate for a 30-year, fixed mortgage has more than doubled this year and last week moved north of 7% for the first time in more than two decades. That\xe2\x80\x99s deterred potential buyers, with prices falling from their peaks and sales plummeting.

Disclosure, I have a fairly cheap 30-year fixed-rate mortgage and a Marcus account, and I am not sure I have much of a point here other than to mention\xc2\xa0that. \xe2\x80\x9cMortgages have negative convexity because their duration goes up as interest rates go up, since nobody wants to prepay, refinance or move when rates are high,\xe2\x80\x9d is a thing that I could have told you a year ago, but then it was of sort of academic interest to me. Now I am living it.

Good severance

One funny thing that could happen to you is that your employer could promise to pay you $680,000 over 16 months, meaning about $42,500 per month, but then in the contract, instead of typing \xe2\x80\x9cwe will pay you $680,000 in monthly installments over 16 months,\xe2\x80\x9d they might type \xe2\x80\x9cwe will pay you $680,000 per month for 16 months.\xe2\x80\x9d That would be better, for you, though you\xe2\x80\x99d definitely have to go to court about it. Here is law professor Jeff Lipshaw on a real case:

A Chinese tech company has a U.S. subsidiary. \xc2\xa0The subsidiary hires a senior executive on a fixed term three-year contract. \xc2\xa0Along the way, the parent company\xe2\x80\x99s founder in China, wants the executive fired. \xc2\xa0Negotiations for the separation ensue, in English and in Mandarin, but the contract is in English, drafted by the subsidiary\xe2\x80\x99s outside lawyers in Virginia. \xc2\xa0Drafts get circulated internally, but it\xe2\x80\x99s clear that the executive, at the very least, is going to get the balance of his salary for the remainder of the three-year contract: \xe2\x80\x9cThe Company agrees to make severance payments to you in the form of continuation of your base salary in effect on the Separation Date for sixteen (16) months.\xe2\x80\x9d \xc2\xa0That total would have been $680,000.

Then things go terribly wrong (internally). \xc2\xa0The outside lawyer changes the draft to this: \xe2\x80\x9c[T]he Company agrees to make monthly severance payments to you in the amount of $ _____ for sixteen (16) months.\xe2\x80\x9d \xc2\xa0The in-house gets the drafts and types into the blank \xe2\x80\x9c$680,000\xe2\x80\x9d, creating a putative obligation of $10,880,000.

This went to court and, as of last month, the executive won \xe2\x80\x94 he gets the $11 million \xe2\x80\x94\xc2\xa0though that could still be appealed.

Oh Elon

I am not going to write much about Elon Musk\xe2\x80\x99s plan to charge users of Twitter $8 a month for some vague set of upgrades, but lots of other people have. My favorite might be\xc2\xa0Ben Thompson\xe2\x80\x99s rather bearish post , which argues essentially that Twitter is a very unpleasant product that most people do not want to use, but is extremely appealing and addictive to a small minority of us with significant personality flaws. This makes it a bad advertising business (not enough scale), but possibly a good subscription business (extremely addictive). I don\xe2\x80\x99t really disagree with him but it is depressing.

Things happen

Apollo bought close to a third of assets sold in UK pension fund fire sale . Broken Deal Triggered Currency Losses for Barclays, Deutsche Bank and Citigroup. Goldman Sachs\xe2\x80\x99 Solomon Sees Deal Recovery Possible Next Year. HSBC CFO Ewen Stevenson Sought Top Job Before Surprise Resignation. How a Mysterious China Screenshot Spurred a $450 Billion Rally. Wall Street Firm Makes a $1.8 Billion Bet on Forest Carbon Offset . Before OPEC+ Production Cut , Saudis Heard Objections From a Top Ally, the U.A.E. Global bankers \xe2\x80\x98 very pro-China \xe2\x80\x99, says UBS chair. Wharton, Berkeley, NYU Offering Online M.B.A.s for the First Time. With Pay Ranges of $2 Million, NYC Transparency Law Is Off to a Glitchy Start. Professional Cornhole Has a Cheating Scandal Called BagGate. 'Non-binary' Romeo and Juliet set in Nazi Germany apologises for omitting Jews from casting call.

If you'd like to get\xc2\xa0Money\xc2\xa0Stuff\xc2\xa0in handy email form, right in your inbox, please subscribe at this link . Or you can subscribe to Money Stuff and other great Bloomberg newsletters here . Thanks!

[1] Though he broke the law a little bit in doing it: The law requires him to disclose his ownership in Twitter once it got above 5%, precisely because that disclosure would alert everyone to his interest in buying the whole company, and might make people stop selling to you at market prices. Musk delayed that legally required disclosure, which allowed him to buy more stock cheaply.

[2] I am asserting this as a general rule of thumb, not a legal doctrine. In the US, the legal doctrine sort of comes from two places. One is the rules about tender offers, and particularly the doctrine of a \xe2\x80\x9c creeping tender \xe2\x80\x9d: Generally if you are trying to buy all of a company\xe2\x80\x99s stock in the market, you have disclosure and fairness requirements. Another is the board of directors' fiduciary duties to all shareholders: If you are trying to buy a company in a merger (rather than a tender offer), you have to negotiate with the board, and the board is not going to agree to give a different deal to different shareholders, because they will get sued. In particular there is a lot of Delaware law from the 1980s heyday of hostile takeovers where boards said \xe2\x80\x9cwe worry that this acquirer will get a majority of the stock and then freeze out minority shareholders at a lower price, so we are putting in place a poison pill to block the acquisition,\xe2\x80\x9d and courts were sympathetic to that.

[3] Or the lenders go to the company; there\xe2\x80\x99s a vigorous market for this stuff, and you want to be proactive about being on the winning side.
